coredata.dc:title: Are carbon dots worth the tremendous attention it is getting: Challenges and opportunities
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Saptarshi
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: In the last decade, research on ‘carbon dot’ (CD) has grown exponentially with its exhilarating aspects in biomedical, environmental remediation, energy-related applications, and many others. However, with the rampant pace of publications, few rudimentary questions over this nanoparticle have been overlooked and continued to be unanswered either consciously or unconsciously. Naturally, it is fostering a negative impact on CD research and confining its enormous potential within the laboratory. Herein, we have tried to decode the interconnected factors that are shackling further growth or transition of CD to industry and ultimately to market. Towards this, we have explicated the inconsistent nomenclature, multifarious synthesis precursors and approaches, the ambiguities over purification, and the nature of fluorescence. We have also audited the nanotoxicity issue of CD and elucidated why CD is disparate from its counterparts over nanotoxicity issues. Then we raised questions with a few examples on otherwise so mesmeric applications of CD. In a first of its kind, we have provided an industrial outlook of the CD and delineated paths to break the barrier. Overall, we believe attention to a few fundamental concerns associated with CD will accelerate its transition to mankind. 〈PE-FRONTEND〉

HEK293: human embryonic kidney cells. A549: human alveolar basal epithelial cells. MDA-MB-231: human breast cancer cell line. HepG2: human liver cancer cell line. ARPE-19: retinal pigment epithelial cells. PRP: human embryonic stem cell‐derived photoreceptor cells. PEG: polyethylene glycol. PEI: polyethyleneimine. hUCMSCs: human umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells. TRIS: tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane. HEL-12469: human embryonic lung fibroblasts. RBCs: red blood cells. LC50: lethal concentration 50 (mortality). EC50: median effective concentration (immobilization). BALB/c mice: an albino, immunodeficient inbred strain. BALB stands for Bagg Albino.

head.ce:title: Are carbon dots worth the tremendous attention it is getting: Challenges and opportunities

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, 801103, India,

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the last decade, research on ‘carbon dot’ (CD) has grown exponentially with its exhilarating aspects in biomedical, environmental remediation, energy-related applications, and many others. However, with the rampant pace of publications, few rudimentary questions over this nanoparticle have been overlooked and continued to be unanswered either consciously or unconsciously. Naturally, it is fostering a negative impact on CD research and confining its enormous potential within the laboratory. Herein, we have tried to decode the interconnected factors that are shackling further growth or transition of CD to industry and ultimately to market. Towards this, we have explicated the inconsistent nomenclature, multifarious synthesis precursors and approaches, the ambiguities over purification, and the nature of fluorescence. We have also audited the nanotoxicity issue of CD and elucidated why CD is disparate from its counterparts over nanotoxicity issues. Then we raised questions with a few examples on otherwise so mesmeric applications of CD. In a first of its kind, we have provided an industrial outlook of the CD and delineated paths to break the barrier. Overall, we believe attention to a few fundamental concerns associated with CD will accelerate its transition to mankind. 〈PE-FRONTEND〉

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon, which constitutes the backbone of all forms of life, has always been a blessed companion to mankind. Various nano-carbons, from fullerene, nanodiamond to ultramodern graphene, and carbon nanotubes, have assured wonderful technological advances. In this elite class of carbon nanoforms, “carbon dot” (CD) is the latest inclusion after its serendipitous discovery in 2004 by Xu et al. during the purification of arc-synthesized single-walled carbon nanotubes [1]. CD is a zero-dimensional (0D) quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterial of size below 10 nm having excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties discrete from other carbon nanoparticles. Low cost, facile synthesis, ease of functionalization, excellent water dispersibility, tunable PL, and good physicochemical and photochemical stability have enabled CD to create a niche for itself in nanotechnology through the promise of the myriad of applications. Biomedical and environmental sensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine including drug and gene delivery, catalysis, optoelectronics, solar cells, wastewater treatment, just to name a few. Their remarkable biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity have distinctly outstripped the conventional semiconductor quantum dots. Amid the unrestrained enthusiasm, some serious challenges like inadequate and unsubstantiated purification, low quantum yield, confusing PL mechanism, inconsistency and ambiguity in deciphering the structure and composition, etc. are standing at the forefront that begs attention sooner than later.

Although it has been over one and a half-decade now since the discovery, CDs got the cock-a-hoop breakthrough with the work of Sun et al. in 2006, where they passivated carbon nanoparticles with diamine-terminated oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG1500N) to attain excellent PL [2]. Since then, the number of publications on CDs per year has sprung up almost exponentially. A search with keywords in SciFinder like “carbon dot, “Carbon Quantum Dot” and “Graphene Quantum Dot” in August 2021 yields ∼14,500 publications along with ∼3700 patents and ∼450 review articles. This depicts how much captivation the CDs have embraced in a relatively short time. Several excellent review articles deserve special mention that has appraised the domain of CDs systematically. Lim et al. surveyed the synthesis, biomedical and catalytic applications of CDs along with some discourse on challenges [3]. Haque et al. discussed the synthesis, physical properties, and energy, biomedical and environmental applications of CDs [4]. In two different studies, Yan et al. and Tian et al. covered the synthesis, functionalization, and specific applications of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) [5,6]. Few reviews have focussed on the synthetic aspects of CDs [7] through metal doping [8], microwave-assisted [9], sonochemical assisted [10], biomass derivation [11], and green methodology [12]. Qu et al. discussed the mechanism of formation and chemical structure of CDs [13]. Elsewhere, few review articles have delineated surface modifications of CDs [14–17]. The review by Shi et al. abridged far-red to near-infrared CDs that are particularly interesting for deep tissue penetration and other biomedical applications [18]. Numerous other reviews discoursed the potential bio-applications of CDs[19–26] including bioimaging [27–29], nanobiosensing [30], food analysis [31,32], and antimicrobials [33,34]. The photocatalytic [35–38] and organocatalytic activity [39] of CDs are also reviewed with good insight. Reviews on CDs for applications in optoelectronics and energy devices [40–42], electronics [43], sensing [44,45], membrane technology [46] are being very informative. Thus, with the availability of copious reviews on several aspects of CDs, it may be apparent that an additional review on CDs hardly envisages anything out of the contemporary. However, there are several issues and concerns with CDs that have been frequently overlooked or remain unanswered either consciously or unconsciously lending a negative impact on the CD research. This review is an attempt to put forward those issues and concerns allied to the CDs.

With the possibility of CDs getting ready for “from lab to market” translation, scrutiny on their state-of-art is of immense importance. To start with, we tried to explicate the puzzling nomenclature of CDs in this review. Since countless strategies have been reported now and then to synthesize CDs, we audited those approaches to draft the imperative need for more scientific rigor, especially with the purification steps. Then, we dug into the origin of PL of CDs and tried to put forward the anomalies in deciphering the mechanistic aspects of PL. We also scrutinized the nanotoxicity issues of CDs that are key to their projected applications especially biomedical. We then upstretched criticism towards otherwise so beguiling applications of CD. We also furnished an industrial viewpoint of CD and provided insights into how it can reach the market. Overall, we have tried to critically decipher, whether CDs are worth such voluminous attention or not, or is it over-research that is underrating and undermining the real potential of the CDs.

Over the years, this fluorescent nanocarbon with varying extent of crystallinity and structural assemblies has received nearly a dozen of names: Carbon Quantum Dot (CQD) [47], GQD [48], CD [49], carbon nanodot (CND) [50], C-dot [51], graphene carbon nanoparticle (GCN) [52], polymer nanodot [53], carbon polymer dot [54], carbonized polymer dot [55], carbogenic dot [56], carbonaceous dot [57], graphitic carbon nitride dot [58], graphitic carbon nitride quantum dot [59], carbon-based quantum dot [60], nitrogen-rich quantum dot [61], fluorescent carbon nanoparticle [62], etc. Such willy-nilly classification and arbitrarily given names or acronyms sometimes provide a false impression that a novel nanomaterial has been introduced [63,64]. At the fore, we need to pin down a generalized nomenclature to avoid confusion among the scientific community.

Materials where all the dimensions are measured in the nanoscale, i.e., no dimension is larger than 100 nm are called zero-dimension (0D) materials. In nanoscience, a “dot” is a nanometer-sized object or particle, whereas a “Quantum dot” (QD) is a semiconductor nanostructure in the quantum confined regime. Considering these terminologies, we would like to suggest a general name “carbon dot” or simply CD for these fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, which was first coined by Sun et al. [2]. Cayuela et al. have suggested sub-categories in the CDs, viz. CNDs, CQDs, and GQDs (Fig. 1 A) [63]. CNDs are amorphous quasi-spherical fluorescent nanocarbon that lack quantum confinement. When the fluorescent nanocarbon exhibit quantum confinement and somewhat crystallinity, they can be referred to as CQDs and GQDs according to their shape. CQDs are spherical or quasi-spherical, a mixture of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon, whereas GQDs are π conjugated single sheet or multiple layers of graphene flakes with the obvious dominance of sp2 carbon. GQDs have been synthesized mostly by the top-down approach, whereas there is a definite inclination towards the bottom-up approach for the synthesis of CQDs. However, few researchers are against the random use of the term ‘quantum’ due to a lack of conviction in quantum confinements through experimentation [37].

Polymer dots, the nano-assembly of π-conjugated polymers, are typically prepared via nano-precipitation, nano-emulsification, or self-assembly, and feature few characteristics similar to that of CDs, such as decent optical properties, high photo-stability, good biocompatibility, etc. [65]. Considering the structural insight (Fig. 1B) and method of preparations, it is undoubtedly noticeable that polymer dots and any sub-category of CDs are a completely different entity. Hence, we would like to discourage the names like ‘polymer nanodot’, ‘carbon polymer dot’, and ‘carbonized polymer dot’ to pronounce any type of CD. The circumstance is similar with the names like ‘graphitic carbon nitride dot’, and ‘graphitic carbon nitride quantum dot’. Graphitic carbon nitride, g-C3N4 is a tri-s-triazine patterned polymeric material consisting of C, N, and few H and has excellent thermal stability, chemical stability, and catalytic properties [66]. N-doped CDs, e.g., CDs prepared via the thermal treatment of citric acid, and urea, thiourea, or any other N-source cannot be compared with the structural craft of the g-C3N4 (Fig. 1C), and hence we should also deter from the usage of the term ‘graphitic carbon nitride’ to avert further fallacy. On the other hand ‘carbon nanoparticle’ is a generalized term to describe nano-sized carbon structures [67,68]. Most importantly, the term has been coined much before the discovery of CD. We feel defining CD by ‘fluorescent carbon nanoparticle’ may create confusion and hence should be avoided. However, if any fluorescent carbon nanoparticles don't possess a well-defined nanostructure or are somewhat larger (>20 nm) than the usual size of CDs, then the term ‘fluorescent carbon nanoparticle’ can be used [69,70]. Here, we are proposing a nomenclature for CDs, where we embolden the use of abbreviated names of raw materials or precursors used for the synthesis of CDs as a subscript to CD (Table 1 ). As numbers of precursors are increasing day by day, we expect that this proposed nomenclature will afford an additional judgment at a glance to a CD, and will terminate delusion with unsystematic nomenclatures. Apart from that, we all know that the structure and properties of carbon dots are not only controlled by precursor molecules but also affected by many other factors, such as temperature, reaction time, pressure, solvent, reaction mode, and so on. Future direction is warranted to address in what form should this information be reflected in the naming.

An overabundance of synthesis approaches within a very short period leads to a few uncorroborated claims without comprehending the factual details. In many instances, the successful formation of CD was perceived by observing the fluorescence or browning of the concerned materials in solution. The chemical transformations occurring during CD synthesis with many bottom-up approaches can be paralleled to those transpiring during food processing, such as roasting coffee, malting of grains, frying onions, or caramelizing sugars, where several complex transformations result in browning and formation of numerous compounds [64]. Consequently, the formation of CDs should not be adjudged by browning now and then. In some instances, few have chosen to believe that the formation of CDs from small organic molecules with mild thermal treatment or even at room temperature is possible somewhat more competently or with better quantum yield [126–129]. It is beyond practicality how a harsh procedure like carbonization was achieved for the formation of a nano-carbon entity in a condition quite similar to cooking at one hundred and some degrees Celsius for an hour or so [130,131]. In fact, if this was possible, we would have enjoyed the taste of CD from our daily foods or simply from a cup of coffee or tea. Rather, at this temperature, such precursors yield highly fluorescent materials that are polymeric or molecular in nature whereas higher temperature is needed for the formation of carbogenic core, albeit at the cost of lower quantum yield [13,131–134]. Surface passivation, heteroatom doping, adding H2O2 during the pyrolysis process, etc. are reported strategies for the improved quantum yield of CDs [92,119,135–137]. Conversely, with our hands-on experience with a few hundreds of differently formulated syntheses of CDs, we have not found any universal correlation. On the other hand, thermal treatments have also been followed in many organic synthesis procedures. As an example, direct thermal treatment of phthalic anhydride and resorcinol at 180 °C in presence of anhydrous ZnCl2 or concentrated H2SO4 yields a very familiar fluorescent dye, Fluorescein. Thus, one cannot deny the possibility of the formation of molecular fluorophores during the thermal treatment of organic matters as a part of the synthesis of CD [130,138]. Moreover, if a CD has been prepared from biomasses other than pure chemicals, the CD formation process becomes more complex, increasing the possibilities of numerous impurities. Undoubtedly, the formation of side-products during CD synthesis is almost inevitable, which calls for the necessity of proper purification processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification of CD: confusion with methods
ce:section.ce:para: Purification is one of the integral parts of any synthesis approach. However, for CD, it is one of the most disputed facets. A bunch of techniques such as centrifugation, syringe filtration, column chromatography, dialysis, solvent extraction, gel electrophoresis, etc. has been exercised for the purification of CD (Fig. 3 A) [139,140]. However, in many cases, they failed to impart adequate standardization that is a prerequisite to further downstream experiments. Few CDs reported even have given these purification procedures a miss, either knowingly or unknowingly. Essner et al. nicely explained the matter by using a cluster of dialysis membranes of different molecular weight cut off (Fig. 3B). They demonstrated how molecular fluorophores generated during the bottom-up synthesis, dominated the fluorescence emission from the CD samples [64]. Although centrifugation or syringe filtration provides good primary separation, they are not sufficient to remove the precursors and putative molecular by-products that leave the purity and consistency of the nanomaterial questionable. With column chromatography, how the researchers selected the CD-containing fractions are hardly mentioned in the research articles [141,142]. We understand that most of the researchers selected the fractions based on typical thin-layer chromatography (TLC) as practiced during any organic synthesis. Nevertheless, a TLC analysis cannot verify whether the new spot is from CD or any molecular fluorophore. Additionally, as separation by column chromatography is based on the polarity of any entity, a CD particle and a molecule with the same overall polarity may elute at the same time. Thus, the random use of column chromatography for the purification of any CD without proper judgment could render the purification process of CDs a failure. The same applies to solvent extraction or liquid-liquid partitioning also.

There are only a few reports where powder X-ray diffraction (pXRD), transmission electron microscope (TEM), steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, etc. have been used to analyze different column chromatographic fractions of CDs [141,142]. pXRD provides a good insight into crystalline or non-crystalline nature and the extent of graphitization of the CD samples. Raman spectroscopy unveils the degree of graphitization [144]. But, both pXRD and Raman spectroscopy cannot divulge whether any molecular fluorophore in the CD sample is present or not. Electron microscopy techniques like transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) may be rendered inconclusive as the imaging area under consideration and demonstration is highly user-dependent. Spectroscopic techniques such as UV–Vis absorption or steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy cannot also conclusively disclose the presence or absence of impurities within the CD samples. For example, red-light emitting CDs from different plant parts have been reported that are bound to contain different molecular fluorophores like porphyrins, polyphenols, etc. Some of the phytochemicals are even red-light emitting, and their presence or absence in the reported CDs is not clear [89,145]. Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) is another tool exercised for the purification of CDs [146]. It extracts different fractions from a raw CD solution based on the size, where smaller particles or molecules permeate through the stationary phase and larger particles move through the column with the mobile phase. However, here aqueous solution has been used as the mobile phase and the stationary phase material (Sephadex) is comparatively costly, which limits its widespread usage. In a few other reports, different fractions of CDs were separated through gel electrophoresis and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Fig. 3C and D) [76,141,143,147]. Although gel electrophoresis provides a good insight about CD fractions based on size to charge ratio, the scale-up of the separation process is a great problem even for the mg scale. But gel electrophoresis can extend a virtuous prospect about the purification status of a CD solution. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) [148] or preparative HPLC are better choices for this scaled-up operations although at the cost of increased complicacy. In our laboratory, we developed a reverse-phase HPLC (RP-HPLC) protocol for the analysis of CDs which was quite successful in verifying the uniformity and purity of CDs [72,119]. During the development of the HPLC profile for those CDs, we witnessed a substantial impact of the buffer composition, buffer concentration, and pH on the separation of the fractions of CDs. Additionally, we conceive that an HPLC integrated with both the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence detector can render a decent outlook about the carbonized part, molecular fluorophore, or any possible polymeric point within a CD solution.

After the above deliberations, it is not practically hard to comprehend that the standard purification process for CDs is yet to be established. A single tool alone cannot satisfy the purification standardization of complex CDs. Primarily, to eliminate large agglomerates or large carbonized particles, centrifugation and/or syringe filtration, and to exclude water-soluble unreacted precursors and molecular by-products, dialysis serves virtuous for the majority of CDs (Fig. 4 ). If the precursors are not water-soluble, then the CDs which are generally good water-dispersible can be separated out from impurities by extracting with water. However, for typical photo-physical studies, fine separations such as gel electrophoresis, size exclusion chromatography, or sucrose density gradient centrifugation hold the key. Elimination of impurities from the synthesized CD through multistep purification process including dialysis and gel electrophoresis has enabled us to divulge an interesting Aggregation induced emission in CD through the addition or eliminations of protons [149]. This shows that although multistep purification can compromise quantum yield, it is worth finding interesting chemical phenomena in CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Is there any blemish in the photoluminescence?
ce:section.ce:para: The PL properties of CD are irrefutably one of the fascinating stuff which makes them an exclusive class among the nano-carbon family. The photo-bleaching effect, one of the major drawbacks associated with fluorescent dyes, is nearly surmounted in the case of CD, thanks to its graphitic core (although partial graphitization). On the contrary, there are few aspects of the PL behavior of CD that intensely cofounded the research community.

The property which made the CDs unique from their nano-carbon counterparts is the PL of CDs (Fig. 5 A). However, insight into the PL properties of CDs flummoxed the researchers since its discovery and yet to be conciliated clearly. Although several spectroscopic techniques such as steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, time-resolved fluorescence anisotropy, time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy, transient absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence correlation spectroscopy, etc. have been exercised to decode the fluorescence origin of the CDs, all failed to attain a comprehensive feat, maybe because of so much assortment of precursors [12,27,63,76,150–154]. After appraising several mechanisms proposed so far, we are discoursing them here concisely.

Since the term ‘quantum’ is integrated within the name of CD (CQD and GQD), we are first explaining the quantum confinement effect. The Bohr radius of an exciton, formed due to the Coulombic interaction of an electron-hole pair, is the distance between an electron and a hole within an exciton. If the size of material shrinks to the Bohr radius of the exciton (< 10 nm), the motion of the electrons and holes are strongly curbed to the dimension of that material and strong quantum confinement predominates resulting in unique optical properties. Here, the fluorescence emission band should be narrow and excitation independent. As the size of the nanoparticles decreases, the excitonic transition energy, and bandgap energy increase, which results in a blue shift of the emission wavelength, and attenuation of the radiationless energy loss [154,155]. However, the quantum confinement within CD is yet to be attested through direct experimentation. Also, the fluorescence emission band of CD is wide and both the excitation dependent and excitation independent fluorescence emission have been reported, which is attributed to uniformity in the surface states of the CD and extent of heterogeneity in the size of the CD.

Secondly, due to the presence of surface defects, functional groups, adsorbed molecules, or impurities in the CD solution, trap states are generated within the bandgap where the photoexcited electrons and holes can be trapped, and following their recombination fluorescence emission takes place. In this phenomenon, the material typically exhibits excitation-dependent fluorescence which shifts systematically towards longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increases.

Thirdly, the fluorescence behavior is more analogous to that observed in metal nanoclusters [156]. Here, the CD fluorescence originates from the superposition of different emitters such as molecular fluorophores, multi-chromophore groups, etc. present in the solution [157]. No clear lattice spacing in high-resolution TEM image was visible here.

Lastly, defining the fluorescence of CDs in one mechanism is not feasible. Instead, their PL is an ensemble of a few mechanisms based on the precursors and synthesis approaches. Although various reports tried to explicate the red or blue shift emission based on the population of functional groups or N, S doping, each of such explanations may apply only to a handful of CDs. Elsewhere, the photoluminescence property of CDs was described with the HOMO-LUMO bandgap as a function of sp2 hybridization (Fig. 5B) [158]. However, hardly any general correlation could be drawn due to the extreme multifariousness of CDs. The excitation-independent fluorescence emission arises from the uniform surface states of the CD, whereas the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission is attributed to the polydispersity in particle size of the CD. As the smaller-sized particles have a large bandgap that requires greater excitation energy, they could not be excited at higher excitation wavelengths. Consequently, the fluorescence emission at shorter wavelengths is lost and the overall emission peak is red-shifted.

If the CD fluoresces towards a higher wavelength region, the solvent used in the study often plays a critical role (Fig. 5C). The solvatochromic effect of CD was explained mostly through the H-bonding theory [78]. Only a few reports additionally brought the Lippert-Mataga and Kamlet-Taft model into the equation [160,161]. In polar protic solvents, the fluorescence was monitored by H-bonding between surface functional groups on the CD and the solvent molecules. Whereas in aprotic solvents, dipole moments (interactions at the excited state), Kamlet-Taft parameters, refractive index, and permittivity of the solvent molecules have been accounted to elucidate the fluorescence behavior of the CD [160,161]. Likewise, the excited states of CD are more stabilized in aprotic solvents yielding better quantum yield. However, considering too much diversity of CD precursors, rigorous studies elucidating the solvatochromic behavior of CD are far away from adequacy. Still, no reports explicated why the fluorescence of red-light emitting CDs gets diminished or vanished completely in an aqueous solution. We understand that laborious investigations, illuminating the solvent effect on the fluorescence of CDs through different solvatochromic models can lessen the confusion and enhance our understanding of the PL behavior of CDs.

Inner filter effect (IFE) is a well-known phenomenon in fluorescence studies, which previously riddled scientists a lot [162]. Although the distortions due to IFE at a high optical density of any fluorescent materials are well documented in the literature, for CDs it has eluded the attention of many researchers. Just by visualizing the red fluorescence of concentrated CD solutions under UV illumination, few researchers credulously claimed the generation of the highly coveted red-light emitting CDs. Such cases originate from the secondary IFE where self-absorption of blue edge photons causes the red-shifted emission (Fig. 5D) [64]. Unfortunately, such IFE has been explained in the light of interparticle distance, where it has been hypothesized that a decrease in inter CD distance results in red emission [163].

With the ever-increasing health and environmental concerns, the translation of many promising nanomaterials is stalled over the nanotoxicity issues. Likewise, the CD has to go through the acid test of nanotoxicity before standing out to its great promise. Different kinds of cell lines, tissues, and organisms have been cast with the CDs to determine the possible toxic effects of CD (Table 3 , Fig. 6 A). After assessing several literature reports, we noted that CD possesses minimal toxicity at the low workable concentration (usually < 100 µg/mL). However, the surface charge of the CD is most critical although the size and shape of the CD are also important in determining the toxicity issue (Fig. 6B). In general, the positively charged CD possesses the highest toxicity, whereas the neutral and negatively charged CD is the most biocompatible [164]. In fact, few negatively charged CD has been found to be well tolerated by human cells with excellent ability to identify bacterial viability and destroy resistant bacteria [165,166]. On the other hand, the size and shape of the CD could influence their macrophage uptakes and consequently, their circulation half-life, excretion path, and interaction with various tissues. The tiny size of CD makes them an excellent candidate to be excreted mostly through urine. Thus, chances of long-term accumulation induced nanotoxicity that is frequently associated with many other nanoparticles are very less for CDs. Due to their excellent water dispersibility, CDs do not tend to precipitate in biological systemic flow, which makes them quite free from aggregation-induced toxicity, unlike their nano-carbon neighbours [118]. Also, in disparity to their naming counterpart QD, CDs do not exert heavy metal-induced toxicity obviously due to the absence of any such metals. One thing which is worth mentioning is that CD has been found to be generated during food processing [167–169], thus we are unknowingly consuming CDs from ancient times without any reported adverse effects which once again back their biocompatibility. However, few studies had also delimited concerns with some CD-induced toxicities [170–173]. In an important study, Lie et al. have shown photodegraded products and cytotoxicity profiles of CDs to prove that photodegradation-induced cytotoxicity is likely common to CDs regardless of their chemical composition [174]. Quite unfortunately, there are only a handful of reports on the degradability of CD (Fig. 6C) [91,175–177], for which declaring CD as a completely biocompatible or non-toxic material may be premature. However, we assume that similar to their nano-carbon counterparts, CDs can be degraded by different microbes (Bacteria: Burkholderia kururiensis, Delftia acidovorans, Stenotrophomonas maltophilia, white-rot fungi: Phanerochaete chrysosporium) and enzymes (lactoperoxidase, manganese peroxidase, horseradish peroxidase, myeloperoxidase, eosinophil peroxidase) [178–180]. Continuous photolysis of various CDs for eight (8) days can degrade CDs up to ∼28–60%, which directly correlates the role of light in CD degradation [174]. Lastly, we want to warn that the decent biocompatibility of CD should not be taken for granted. The toxicity may vary from one CD to another and at least one in vitro or in vivo test should be performed before tagging any CD as nontoxic. However, thorough in vitro and in vivo studies are needed before commercializing a particular CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Are the applications of CDs really mesmeric?
ce:section.ce:para: Potential applications of CDs in the laboratory milieu have propagated to almost every possible framework of material science (Chart 1 ). In biomedical, combining the diagnostic and therapeutic facets, CDs could truly prompt the creation of a comprehensive nanodrug. In environmental and energy contexts, the CD has great potential thanks to its large surface area, decent charge carrier extraction or separation, abundant active sites, excellent light absorptivity, and integrability with other materials [5]. It is worth mentioning here that the red-emission of (> 600 nm) CDs is getting increasing attention in biomedical applications due to their little damage to the biological matrix, deep tissue penetration, and minimum autofluorescence background of biosamples. Precursors like Phenylenediamines and Polythiopenes may gain importance for obtaining such CDs [18]. However, the tempting applications of CD also are not free from doubts. For example, Aspirin is an excellent anti-inflammatory drug, Zingiberis rhizome has an analgesic effect, and metformin accumulates in mitochondria. However, it is not clear from those reports, whether the anti-inflammatory, analgesic, or mitochondria targeting effects of aspirin, Zingiberis rhizome, and metformin-derived CDs originate from the respective CDs or residual precursors present on those CDs [97,199,200]. In a different study, it has been shown that the retentate after dialysis is more sensitive towards metal sensing than as-synthesized CD, where the molecular by-products are masking the true fluorescence response of that CD [64]. The photosensitizer CD has also been described as a potent antioxidant agent which is not awkward considering the presence of light during the photosensitizing activity of the CD and the absence of light during the antioxidant activity of the CD [201,202]. But, surprisingly the antioxidant activity of CD has also been highlighted in applications where exposure of the CD-containing material to light is quite inevitable [203]. In another instance, the CD has been projected as a UV absorber in sunscreen even after several reports on the ROS generation capacity of CD under sunlight [204]. Elsewhere, few reports portrayed CD as a carrier of the drug, where competent drug loading on CD surface through non-covalent interactions was accomplished [109,128,205–207]. However, it is not explicated, how a drug molecule like doxorubicin with size ∼1.5 nm was loaded with great efficiency on a particle with size ∼5 nm. Unfortunately, in many instances, the researchers have been left midway without going deep into the mechanism of the application and have caused confusion with the newbie CD. The above descriptions are only a few examples.

The underlying intention of any material research is to introduce smarter materials for state-of-the-art applications and ultimately to deliver them to mankind through its industrial production. Towards this, the scaled-up synthesis is probably the biggest common barrier from the scientific and technological point of view. At the same time, the other limitations associated with CD are needed to be addressed by the researcher to make the CD competent for industrialization. Additionally, there exist applications specific hurdles. For example, fluorescent sensing mandate CD with high quantum yield, for nanomedicine applications biocompatibility is the primary requirement, catalytic applications desire a low bandgap, few hard polymeric applications crave for enhanced mechanical property, etc. However, it is heartening to witness that the scale-up process for CD has already been underway for the last few years. Reports on the synthesis of CD in kilogram scale [82,303] synthesis of CD via Continuous Hydrothermal Flow Synthesis approach [304] are few examples towards the industrial scale-up process. Encouragingly, companies like Dotz Nano Ltd., Green Science Alliance Co., Ltd., ZEN Graphene Solutions Ltd. have started commercialization or teamed up with another specialized company for the same. They are currently playing in the area of anti-counterfeiting, inkjet ink, LEDs, lithium-ion battery anode, etc. According to media reports, anti-counterfeiting tags from Dotz Nano Ltd. were used in the recently concluded Tokyo Olympic Games 2020. Future Markets, Inc. has also published a global market report 2020–2030 on CD in December 2020 estimating a huge potential [305]. However, we have not found any commercialization reports or news on biomedical applications of CD including clinical trials that once again remind us of the unresolved riddles on CD.

One of the major reasons for the spurt in CD research is the cheap, easy availability, and broad substrate scope that is essentially related to the economics of the research. However, there is a growing negative impact on CD research. This is due to the race for expedited publication leading to over-excitement where we are witnessing a disheartening trend of unwillingness to mechanistically dive deep. Quite unfortunately, only a handful of researchers or scientists have warranted the need for extra-scientific rigor against such detrimental, unsubstantiated, and uncritical claims or fallacies (Fig. 7 ) [63,64,130,138]. The intrinsic problem with CDs is batch-to-batch and lab-to-lab reproducibility, along with the vagueness in purification protocols. Therefore, consistency in synthesis, purification, characterization, and to some extent nomenclature should be of foremost importance to preserve the verve in the CD research or to stand up to the ululation about the immense potential of the CD.

Growing evidence suggests that the defects on CD have a significant role in their optical and electronic properties. Hence, approaches that can engineer the location and population of the defects on CD are critical to its synthesis. Attention should also be on precise control over the size, shape, and functionalities of the CD as well as on synthesis scale up for commercialization with the same precision. On the other hand, CD with high quantum yield, red/near-infrared (NIR) responding CD are always coveted but should not be a result of delusion. CDs have shown glimpses to lessen the burden on the environment either through its catalytic activity or by energy storage applications and now research is desired in this area on the mechanistic aspect and precise fabrication. A thorough knowledge about the fate of the CD within a human body and in the environment should also be there before its commercialization. Integration of theoretical or computational studies on CD with the experimentation is expected to render a good insight, where the formation of structural models is the biggest challenge due to heavy uncertainty and complexity with the CD structure [306,307]. Nevertheless, it is heartening to witness that the CD is receiving research interest in diverse areas far away from the traditional applications of a fluorescence entity and semiconductor. More encouragingly, the hardcore material science applications of CD do not possess too many blemishes similar to that of fluorescence facets of CD in the biomedical domain. Even CD-based anti-counterfeit tags, inkjet inks have reached the market. Finally, we believe, greater understanding and resolution of the concerns associated with the CD could free it from the manacles of the laboratory to reach out to humanity with its full potential.
As it is a review manuscript, sharing of raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings is not applicable.
